1: kai egeneto en tō einai auton en topō tini proseuchomenon — one day Jesus was praying in a certain place. The prayer-context is Luke’s consistent framing for moments of teaching on prayer; Jesus prays before teaching about prayer (cf. Luke 3:21; Luke 6:12; Luke 9:18,28). The disciple’s request (kyrie, didaxon hēmas proseuchesthai) echoes John’s teaching of his disciples; the request implies a distinctive prayer practice is part of what defines a rabbi’s school. The Lord’s Prayer that follows is shorter in Luke than in Matthew (Matthew 6:9-13), without the doxology.
2: hotan proseuchēsthe legete: pater, hagiasthētō to onoma sou, elthetō hē basileia sou — when you pray, say: Father, hallowed be your name, your kingdom come. Luke’s version omits Matthew’s hēmōn ho en tois ouranois (our Father in heaven) — simply Pater (Father), the intimate Aramaic Abba. The two kingdom-oriented petitions (hagiasthētō to onoma, let your name be sanctified; elthetō hē basileia, let your kingdom come) are aorist imperatives — definitive, eschatological acts petitioned. Luke’s version also omits Matthew’s “your will be done on earth as in heaven,” though this was likely part of the original prayer.
3: ton arton hēmōn ton epiousion didou hēmin to kath hēmeran — give us each day our daily bread. Epiousion is the most debated single word in the New Testament — it appears only here and in Matthew 6:11 and in a 5th-century papyrus. Three main interpretations: (1) for today (epi tēn ousan hēmeran); (2) for tomorrow (epiousa = the coming day); (3) necessary for existence (from epi + ousia = for subsistence). Jerome’s Latin renders it supersubstantialis (supersubstantial) in Matthew but quotidianum (daily) in Luke. The Eucharistic interpretation (the bread from above) has early patristic support (Origen). The MKT “daily” captures the temporal sense; the spiritual surplus resonates throughout the prayer’s eschatological horizon.
4: kai aphes hēmin tas hamartias hēmōn, kai gar autoi aphiomen panti opheilonti hēmin — forgive us our sins, for we also forgive everyone who sins against us. Luke uses hamartias (sins) where Matthew uses opheilēmata (debts — the original Aramaic hobayn which means both debts and sins). Luke explains the human forgiveness (aphiomen) with kai gar (for indeed, because) — the human practice of forgiving is offered as a ground for divine forgiveness; or more likely, it is the evidence of the divine forgiveness already received. The verb tense in Luke is present (aphiomen = we forgive, ongoing) vs. Matthew’s aorist (we have forgiven).
5: kai eipen pros autous: tis ex hymōn hexei philon kai poreusetai pros auton mesonyktiou — then Jesus said to them: Suppose you have a friend and you go to him at midnight. The Friend at Midnight parable (unique to Luke) is an a fortiori argument from the lesser to the greater: if a reluctant human friend eventually responds to persistent asking, how much more will the willing heavenly Father respond? The midnight timing (mesonyktiou) represents the most inconvenient possible moment; the request for three loaves (treis artous) is for guests who have arrived unexpectedly and must be hosted.
6: epeidē philos mou paredeto ex hodou pros me kai ouk echō ho parathēsō autō — a friend of mine on a journey has come to me, and I have nothing to set before him. The social obligation of hospitality in Mediterranean culture was binding and honor-laden: to have a guest arrive and not be able to offer food was a serious social failure. Ouk echō ho parathēsō autō (I have nothing to set before him) — the needy person is asking not for himself but for a third party; the intercession structure (asking on behalf of another) models the pattern of prayer the chapter is teaching.
7: ho de esōthen apokritheis eipē: mē moi kopous pareche; ēdē hē thyra kekleistai kai ta paidia mou met emou eis tēn koitēn eisin — the one inside answers: Don’t bother me. The door is already locked, and my children and I are in bed. The first-century peasant house had one room; the whole family slept together on a raised platform. Kekleistai (has been shut, perfect tense — firmly, already closed) and the children already in bed make the request maximally inconvenient. The householder’s reluctance is genuine, not theatrical; the parable requires a genuinely reluctant figure for the a fortiori argument to work.
8: dia ge tēn anaideian autou egertheis dōsei autō hosōn chrēzei — because of his shameless audacity he will surely get up and give him as much as he needs. Anaideia (shamelessness, boldness, impudence — a hapax in the NT) is the key word: the persistence that overcomes reluctance. In the honor-shame framework, to keep knocking after being refused once is to risk social humiliation — the asker’s need overcomes his honor-calculation. Some interpreters read anaideia as the householder’s avoidance of shame (getting up to prevent the embarrassment of the neighbor’s continued banging), but the grammatical subject is the asker. Shameless persistence is the parable’s model for prayer.
9: kagō hymin legō: aiteite kai dothēsetai hymin, zēteite kai heurēsete, krouete kai anoigēsetai hymin — I say to you: ask and it will be given to you; seek and you will find; knock and the door will be opened to you. The three verbs in ascending order of intensity: aiteite (ask, request), zēteite (seek, search actively), krouete (knock, persistent insistent action). All three are present imperatives — continuous, ongoing actions. The divine passive in the responses (dothēsetai, anoigēsetai) identifies God as the giver and opener. The logic is the parable’s conclusion applied universally: what the midnight asking secured from a reluctant friend, the asking-seeking-knocking secures from the willing Father.
10: pas gar ho aitōn lambanei kai ho zētōn heuriskei kai tō krouonti anoigēsetai — for everyone who asks receives; the one who seeks finds; and to the one who knocks, the door will be opened. The universal promise (pas = everyone) is stated as a present-tense axiomatic truth: the asking-and-receiving, seeking-and-finding, knocking-and-opening are presented as reliable correspondences, not occasional outcomes. The three pairs form a complete hermeneutic of prayer: it is asking-based, searching-active, and door-oriented (expectant of access to the divine presence).
11: tina de ex hymōn ton patera aitēsei ho huios ichthyn kai anti ichthyos ophin autō epidōsei — which of you fathers, if your son asks for a fish, will give him a snake instead? The argument from normal parental love to divine giving employs the fish-snake and egg-scorpion contrasts (Luke’s version differs slightly from Matthew’s). The fish and snake, the egg and scorpion, are similar in external appearance (the serpent might be mistaken for a fish; the scorpion egg-shaped); the parable exploits the similarity to underscore the contrast. A father would never exploit the similarity to harm his child.
12: ē kai aitēsei ōon kai epidōsei autō skorpion — or if he asks for an egg, will give him a scorpion? The egg-scorpion comparison is Luke’s unique addition to the fish-snake pair. Epidōsei (will give in response, will hand over to) — the verb emphasizes the deliberateness of the giving; no father would deliberately substitute a harmful creature for a nourishing one. The a fortiori argument prepares for Luke 11:13: if imperfect, sinful fathers give good gifts, the perfect Father gives infinitely better.
13: ei oun hymeis ponēroi hyparchontes oidate domata agatha didonai tois teknois hymōn, posō mallon ho patēr ex ouranou dōsei pneuma hagion tois aitousin auton — if you then, though you are evil, know how to give good gifts to your children, how much more will your Father in heaven give the Holy Spirit to those who ask him! Luke’s climax differs from Matthew’s (“good things”): here the supreme gift is pneuma hagion (the Holy Spirit). Luke’s Pentecostal theology shapes his version of the prayer-chapter: the ultimate answer to prayer, the best gift the Father can give, is the Holy Spirit — the fulfilment of the kingdom’s promise (Acts 1:4-5; Luke 2:38). Posō mallon (how much more — the standard kal vaḥomer intensifier) makes the gift immeasurably greater than any human father’s.
14: kai ēn ekballōn daimonion, kai auto ēn kōphon — Jesus was driving out a demon that was mute. The exorcism (unique to Luke and Matthew in this form; different from Mark’s setting) produces speech in one who was mute (kōphos, deaf-mute). The crowd’s amazement (ethaumasan hoi ochloi) is the expected response; the divided responses (accusation of Beelzebul, demand for a sign from heaven) that follow represent the opposite of amazement — hostile re-categorization of the miracle as demonic power.
15: tines de ex autōn eipon: en Beelzeboul tō archonti tōn daimoniōn ekballei ta daimonia — some of them said: By Beelzebul, the prince of demons, he is driving out demons. Beelzeboul — the name derives from “lord of the house” (ba’al zebul) or “lord of dung/flies” (ba’al zebub, 2 Kings 1:2); the latter is a contemptuous distortion of the Philistine god’s name. The accusation is the most serious possible: attributing Jesus’s exorcistic power not to God but to the chief of demons. This is the context of the Blasphemy against the Holy Spirit saying (Luke 12:10).
16: heteroi de peiraszontes sēmeion ex ouranou ezētoun par autou — others tested him by asking for a sign from heaven. Peiraszontes (testing — the same root as the Devil’s temptation in Luke 4:2, peirazomenos) — the demand for a sign is hostile, not genuinely seeking. Sēmeion ex ouranou (a sign from heaven, a celestial sign) — the demand for a spectacular supernatural proof, over and above the exorcism just performed, is a refusal to accept the present evidence. Jesus will respond with the sign of Jonah (Luke 11:29): the only sign that will be given is the sign of a death and resurrection.
17: autos de eidōs autōn ta dianoēmata eipen autois — Jesus knew their thoughts and said to them. Again Luke notes Jesus’s knowledge of unspoken thoughts (cf. Luke 6:8; Luke 9:47) — a recurring Lukan marker of divine perceptiveness. The house-divided argument that follows is a logical reductio: if Satan is expelling his own demons, he is destroying his own kingdom. The argument assumes that Satan’s kingdom is a coherent organized domain, not a chaos; its internal consistency is exactly what the divided-house scenario would undermine.
18: ei de kai ho Satanas eph heauton diemeristhē, pōs stathēsetai hē basileia autou — if Satan is divided against himself, how can his kingdom stand? Ho Satanas (the Adversary, the Accuser — the Hebrew śāṭān as a proper name for the cosmic opponent) has a kingdom (basileia) — the rival ordering of reality under the power of darkness. Jesus’s logic: internal division produces collapse; if the exorcisms were demonic, Satan is self-destructing. The parallel structure of Satan’s kingdom and God’s kingdom is implicit throughout; the exorcisms represent territorial conflict at the cosmic level.
19: ei de egō en Beelzeboul ekballō ta daimonia, hoi huioi hymōn en tini ekballousin — now if I drive out demons by Beelzebul, by whom do your followers drive them out? Jewish exorcists practiced exorcism within the covenant community (Acts 19:13-14 shows the practice). Dia touto autoi hymōn kritai esontai (so then, they will be your judges) — Jesus turns the accusation’s logic against the accusers: if they accept Jewish exorcism as legitimate, they cannot categorize Jesus’s exorcisms as demonic without condemning their own community’s practice.
20: ei de en daktylō theou ekballō ta daimonia, ara ephthasen eph hymas hē basileia tou theou — but if I drive out demons by the finger of God, then the kingdom of God has come upon you. Daktylō theou (finger of God) — where Matthew reads “Spirit of God” (Matthew 12:28), Luke uses “finger of God” — the LXX phrase used by Pharaoh’s magicians when they recognized the plagues as divine action (Exodus 8:19) and for the writing of the Ten Commandments (Exodus 31:18; Deuteronomy 9:10). The Exodus resonance is deliberate: the exorcisms are the new plagues, liberating the captives of the new Pharaoh (Satan). Ephthasen (has arrived, come upon — aorist of anticipatory arrival) is Luke’s strongest “realized eschatology” statement.
21: hotan ho ischyros kathōplismenos phylassē tēn heautou aulēn — when a strong man, fully armed, guards his own house, his possessions are safe. The strong man parable (ho ischyros, the strong/mighty one) depicts Satan as an armed householder guarding his domain. Kathōplismenos (fully armed, equipped with full armor — a military term) and ta huparchonta autou en eirēnē estin (his possessions are in peace) — the ironic use of eirēnē (peace) for the security of Satan’s domain. The exorcisms are not Satan expelling his own forces but the stronger one (ho ischyroteros) overpowering the strong one.
22: epan de ho ischyroteros autou epelthōn nikēsē auton — but when someone stronger attacks and overpowers him. Ho ischyroteros (the stronger one) — the comparative deliberately echoes John the Baptist’s announcement (Luke 3:16 — “one more powerful than I is coming”). Jesus is the stronger one who has come to overpower the strong man. Tēn panoplian autou airei (he takes away his full armor) — panoplia (full armor, the same term in Ephesians 6:11,13 for the Christian’s armor) — the plundering of the strong man’s armor represents the dismantling of Satan’s capacity to defend his domain. The exorcisms are armor-stripping operations.
23: ho mē ōn met emou kat emou estin, kai ho mē synagōn met emou skorporizei — whoever is not with me is against me, and whoever does not gather with me scatters. The binary of gathering and scattering frames the exorcism controversy’s conclusion: there is no neutral ground in the cosmic conflict. Met emou / kat emou (with me / against me) — the prepositions are the only difference; neutrality is logically impossible in a spiritual conflict where inaction is itself an act. The gathering-scattering pair is harvest imagery: the eschatological harvest demands workers who gather; those who do not gather by definition scatter the harvest.
24: hotan to akatharton pneuma exelthē apo tou anthrōpou, dierchetai di anydrou topōn zētoyn anapausin — when an impure spirit comes out of a person, it goes through waterless places seeking rest. The exorcism’s sequel-warning: an expelled demon wanders through arid/waterless places (anydrous topous) — the desert as demonic space (cf. Isaiah 34:14; Bar Luke 4:35; Revelation 18:2). The failure to find rest (anapausin) drives the return to the former host. The warning context (following the Beelzebul controversy) applies this to Israel: the generation that has witnessed exorcisms but refuses to respond is in danger of a worse possession than before.
25: kai elthon heuriskei sesarōmenon kai kekosmēmenon — when it arrives, it finds the house swept clean and put in order. Sesarōmenon (swept) and kekosmēmenon (put in order, adorned) — both perfect participles indicating a completed state. The house is emptied of evil but not filled with good; reformation without regeneration leaves a vacuum. The clean-but-empty house is more vulnerable than an occupied house: the demon can reenter without resistance. The parable applies to religious reform that stops at external correction without the Spirit’s indwelling.
26: tote poreuetai kai paralambanei hetera pneumata ponērotera heautou hepta — then it goes and takes seven other spirits more wicked than itself. Seven spirits (the number of completeness) — the final state is a sevenfold intensification of the first. Hē eschate tēs anthrōpou ekeinēs ginetai cheirōn tēs prōtēs (the final condition of that person is worse than the first) — the warning applies to “this evil generation” (Luke 11:29): having experienced the liberating power of Jesus’s ministry and rejected it, the spiritual vacancy leaves them susceptible to a worse condition than before Jesus came.
27: egeneto de en tō legein auton tauta eparasa phōnēn gynē tis ek tou ochlou eipen autō: makaria hē koilia hē bastasasa se — as Jesus was saying these things, a woman in the crowd called out: Blessed is the womb that bore you. The woman’s beatitude is a maternal blessing — praising Jesus by praising the physical maternity that produced him. Makaria hē koilia (blessed the womb) and hoi mastoi hous ethēlasas (the breasts you nursed from) — the specifically physical blessing is not rejected by Jesus but transcended: the true blessedness is not physical proximity to Jesus but covenant response to his word.
28: autos de eipen: menoun makarioi hoi akouontes ton logon tou theou kai phylassontes — he replied: Blessed rather are those who hear the word of God and obey it. Menoun (rather, on the contrary — a corrective particle that does not negate but reorients) — Jesus does not deny his mother’s blessing but redirects it to a greater beatitude. The hearing-and-keeping (akouontes... kai phylassontes) formulation echoes the Shema structure (hear and keep) and Deuteronomy’s covenant pattern. The biological connection to Jesus is surpassed by the covenant connection to his word; Mary’s truest beatitude is her hearing and obeying (Luke 1:38), not her biological maternity.
29: tōn de ochlōn epathroizomenōn ērxato legein: hē genea hautē genea ponēra estin; sēmeion zētei, kai sēmeion ou dothēsetai autē ei mē to sēmeion Iōna — this is a wicked generation. It asks for a sign, but none will be given it except the sign of Jonah. Hē genea hautē (this generation) — Luke’s characteristic judgment-target. Sēmeion Iōna (the sign of Jonah) — in Matthew, Jesus explains this as Jonah’s three days in the fish = the Son of Man’s three days in the earth. Luke’s version moves differently: the comparison is to Jonah as a sign to the Ninevites (Luke 11:30 — Jonah himself was the sign, not the fish-episode). The preaching that produced Nineveh’s repentance is the parallel to Jesus’s preaching to this generation.
30: kathōs gar egeneto Iōnas tois Nineuītais sēmeion, houtōs estai kai ho huios tou anthrōpou tē genea tautē — for as Jonah was a sign to the Ninevites, so also will the Son of Man be to this generation. Luke’s Jonah-comparison focuses on the prophetic preacher as sign: Jonah’s arrival in Nineveh (a man from the sea, changed by the experience) was itself the sign that moved the city to repentance. Jesus as the Son of Man — the Danielic figure of cosmic authority — is the sign to this generation; his presence, his words, his miracles, and ultimately his death and resurrection are the sign. The generation that fails to respond condemns itself.
31: basilissa notou egerthēsetai en tē krisei meta tōn andrōn tēs geneas tautēs kai katakrinei autous — the Queen of the South will rise at the judgment with the people of this generation and condemn them. The Queen of Sheba (1 Kings 10:1-13) traveled from the ends of the earth to hear Solomon’s wisdom. Idou pleion Solomōnos hōde (and now something greater than Solomon is here) — the comparative pleion (greater, more) is neuter, not masculine: the greater thing is the kingdom and its wisdom, which is present in Jesus. The Gentile queen’s response to lesser wisdom condemns a generation that refuses greater wisdom.
32: andres Nineuī anastēsontai en tē krisei meta tēs geneas tautēs kai katakrinousin autēn — the men of Nineveh will stand up at the judgment with this generation and condemn it. The Ninevites repented at Jonah’s preaching — a single prophet’s message with no miracles, no royal authority. Idou pleion Iōna hōde (and now something greater than Jonah is here) — the double pleion (greater than Solomon, greater than Jonah) brackets the two OT comparison-figures. If Nineveh repented at less and Jerusalem refuses more, the comparison is self-condemning. Judgment by the Gentiles who responded is the inversion the wicked generation deserves.
33: oudeis lychon hapsas eis kryptēn tithēsin — no one lights a lamp and puts it in a place where it will be hidden. The lamp-on-the-stand saying follows the sign-of-Jonah discourse: the light of Jesus’s ministry has not been hidden but publicly proclaimed. Hina hoi eisporeuomenoi to phōs blepōsin (so that those who come in may see the light) — the lamp is for the benefit of those entering the space. The application to Jesus: his ministry is the lamp that the householder has placed on the stand; the question is whether the entering generation has eyes to see it.
34: ho lychnos tou sōmatos estin ho ophthalmos sou; hotan ho ophthalmos sou haplous ē, kai holon to sōma sou phōteinon estin — your eye is the lamp of your body. When your eyes are healthy, your whole body also is full of light. Haplous (single, healthy, generous — the word means both “single/simple” and “sound/healthy”; in the Semitic tradition, the “good eye” means generosity and the “evil eye” means miserliness) and ponēros (evil, sick, unhealthy eye). The spiritual interpretation: the eye as the organ of moral perception determines whether the whole person is illuminated by the light of the kingdom or darkened. The Beelzebul accusers have a diseased eye — they see the works of God and categorize them as demonic.
35: skopei oun mē to phōs to en soi skotos estin — see to it, then, that the light within you is not darkness. The self-examination command (skopei, watch yourself, take heed) is urgent: the danger is not obvious external darkness but internal darkness masquerading as light. The paradox of “the light within you is darkness” applies to those who have religious certainty (the Pharisees) but whose moral perception is inverted: they see demonic in divine action and divine in their own tradition. Self-examination is required precisely by those who are most confident of their light.
36: ei oun to sōma sou holon phōteinon, mē echon meros ti skoteinon, estai holōs phōteinon hōs hotan ho lychnos tē astrapē phōtizē se — if your whole body is full of light, no part of it dark, it will be just as full of light as when a lamp shines its light on you. The positive conclusion: the body fully illuminated is like the moment when a lamp floods the room with complete brightness. The eschatological implication: full illumination is the kingdom’s telos — the comprehensive light that leaves no dark corners. The lamp metaphor closes with the promise of total illumination for those whose eye is haplous.
37: en de tō lalēsai erōta auton pharisaios hopos aristēsē par autō — when Jesus had finished speaking, a Pharisee invited him to eat with him. The second dinner-with-a-Pharisee scene (cf. Luke 7:36-50) provides the setting for the woe-oracles. Luke’s structuring of the woes within a meal-setting is socially pointed: the Pharisee invites Jesus to his table; Jesus responds with a public critique. Eiselthōn de anepesen (he went in and reclined) — Jesus accepts the invitation without social ceremony; the lack of pre-meal hand-washing that follows is the immediate provocation.
38: ho de Pharisaios idōn ethaumasen hoti ou prōton ebaptisthē pro tou aristou — the Pharisee was surprised that Jesus did not first wash before the meal. Ebaptisthē (was baptized, ritually immersed) — the pre-meal immersion washing (not merely hand-washing but a full ritual bath for some Pharisaic schools) was a purity practice going beyond Torah requirements; it was a “tradition of the elders” (cf. Mark 7:3-4). Jesus’s omission is deliberate: the body-cleaning without heart-cleaning is precisely what his following critique addresses.
39: eipen de ho kyrios pros auton: nyn hymeis hoi pharisaioi to exōthen tou potēriou kai tou pinakos katharizete, to de esōthen hymōn gemei harpagēs kai ponērias — the Lord said: Now you Pharisees clean the outside of the cup and dish, but inside you are full of greed and wickedness. The cup-and-dish metaphor makes the critique concrete: the scrupulous cleaning of vessels (to exōthen, the outside) while the inner person (to de esōthen hymōn) is full of harpagē (greed, extortion — the word for seizing property unlawfully) and ponēria (wickedness, evil). The Pharisaic purity system has become an external ritual that conceals and perhaps enables internal moral corruption.
40: aphrones, ouch ho poiēsas to exōthen kai to esōthen epoiēsen — you foolish people! Did not the one who made the outside make the inside also? Aphrones (fools, those without wisdom — a strong address) — the argument from creation: the Creator God who made the exterior of the vessel also made the interior; a theology that attends to the exterior without the interior has missed the Creator’s design. The rhetorical question expects the answer “yes”: of course the Creator made both — which means both are accountable to him.
41: plēn ta enonta dote eleēmosynēn, kai idou panta kathara hymin estin — but now as for what is inside you — be generous to the poor, and everything will be clean for you. The solution to the inside-outside purity problem is not additional external ritual but generosity (eleēmosynēn, almsgiving). Ta enonta (what is inside, what you have within/at hand) — use what you possess for the poor. Luke’s version of the cleanness-teaching makes generosity the measure of purity: the cleaned inside manifests as open-handed giving. The purity system is redirected from ritual compliance to moral transformation expressed in economic sharing.
42: alla ouai hymin tois Pharisaiois, hoti apodekateute to hēdyosmon kai to pēganon kai pan lachanon — woe to you Pharisees, because you give God a tenth of your mint, rue and all other kinds of garden herbs, but you neglect justice and the love of God. Apodekateute (you tithe) — the tithe of garden herbs extended the Torah’s grain-oil-wine tithe (Deuteronomy 14:22-23) to the level of individual stalks of herbs; the meticulous attention to micro-tithing. Parerchesthe tēn krisin kai tēn agapēn tou theou (you pass over justice and the love of God) — krisis (justice, judgment) and agapē tou theou (the love of God — either love toward God or God’s own love enacted through them) are the weightier matters that the herb-tithe has displaced. Luke reads “love of God” where Matthew reads “mercy and faithfulness” (Matthew 23:23).
43: ouai hymin tois Pharisaiois, hoti agapate tēn prōtokathedrian en tais synagōgais kai tous aspasmous en tais agorais — woe to you Pharisees, because you love the most important seats in the synagogues and respectful greetings in the market-places. Prōtokathedria (first seat, seat of honor at the front of the synagogue) and aspasmoi en tais agorais (greetings in the market-places — the ceremonial public greetings of honor expected by religious authorities). The love of public status (agapate) inverts the love-command: the Pharisees who are commanded to love God and neighbor have channeled their love into loving public honor. Status-seeking has replaced covenant-love.
44: ouai hymin, hoti este hōs ta mnēmeia ta adēla kai hoi anthrōpoi hoi peripatountes epanō ouk oisin — woe to you, because you are like unmarked graves, which people walk over without knowing it. Ta mnēmeia ta adēla (unmarked graves, invisible tombs) — in Jewish law, contact with a grave or tomb caused ritual impurity; unmarked graves were especially dangerous because travelers could unknowingly walk over them. The Pharisees are hidden sources of defilement: their outward respectability (adēla, invisible, undetected) conceals the contagion within. Those who come into contact with them — their students, their communities — are unknowingly defiled. Matthew’s parallel reads “whitewashed tombs” (Matthew 23:27); Luke’s unmarked graves emphasize the hidden rather than the hypocritical.
45: apokritheis de tis tōn nomikōn legei autō: didaskale, tauta legōn kai hēmas hybrizeis — one of the experts in the law answered him: Teacher, when you say these things, you insult us also. The legal scholars (nomikoi, experts in the Torah — distinct from Pharisees as a group, though related) recognize that the critique includes them. Hybrizeis (you insult, treat with contempt — the verb of violent public dishonor) — the scholar’s protest that the woes constitute social humiliation. Jesus’s response is to add three more woes specifically for the lawyers.
46: ho de eipen: kai hymin tois nomikois ouai, hoti phortizete tous anthrōpous phortia dysbastakata — and you experts in the law, woe to you, because you load people down with burdens they can hardly carry. Phortia dysbastakata (burdens that are hard to bear, difficult loads) — the halakhic elaborations that the legal scholars had developed (the “fence around the Torah”) had become oppressive weight on ordinary people who could not keep all the interpretive rulings. Autoi de heni tōn daktylōn hymōn ou prospsauete tois fortiois (you yourselves do not touch the loads with one of your fingers) — they impose but do not carry; the scholars are exempt from the practical burdens they assign to others.
47: ouai hymin, hoti oikodomeite ta mnēmeia tōn prophētōn, hoi de pateres hymōn apekteinan autous — woe to you, because you build tombs for the prophets, and it was your ancestors who killed them. The prophet-tomb building is on its surface an act of honor; Jesus’s indictment exposes the irony: building tombs for the prophets one’s ancestors killed is not repentance but monument-making. The descendants inherit the ancestors’ violence while honoring its victims in stone — a gesture that serves the present generation’s self-image rather than repairing the injustice.
48: ara martyres este kai syneudokeite tois ergois tōn paterōn hymōn, hoti autoi men apekteinan autous hymeis de oikodomeite — so you testify that you approve of what your ancestors did; they killed the prophets and you build their tombs. The indictment uses the witnesses’ own action as testimony against them: the tomb-building is taken as evidence of approval (syneudokeite, you agree with, endorse), not honor. The generation that is about to kill Jesus completes the pattern: rejecting the living prophet while honoring the dead ones is the consistent behavior of the prophetic-rejection tradition.
49: dia touto kai hē sophia tou theou eipen: apostelō eis autous prophētas kai apostolous, kai ex autōn apoktenousin kai diōxousin — because of this, God in his wisdom said: I will send them prophets and apostles, some of whom they will kill and others they will persecute. Hē sophia tou theou (the wisdom of God) — a personified divine speech (not a quotation from a specific text; possibly an apocryphal source or a free composite); Jesus may be identifying himself as divine Wisdom speaking here. The sending of prophets and apostles who will be killed and persecuted is both prediction and indictment: the divine patience in sending successive messengers makes the rejection increasingly culpable.
50: hina ekzētēthē to haima pantōn tōn prophētōn to ekkechymenon apo katabolēs kosmou apo tēs geneas tautēs — therefore this generation will be held responsible for the blood of all the prophets that has been shed since the beginning of the world. The “blood of all the prophets” is the cumulative debt of the rejection-and-killing tradition; “since the beginning of the world” (apo katabolēs kosmou) frames the entire span of prophetic history. The generation that kills Jesus becomes the culminating instance — the generation in which the full weight of the accumulated guilt falls because the ultimate messenger has been rejected.
51: apo haimatos Abel heōs haimatos Zachariou tou apolomenou metaxy tou thysiastēriou kai tou oikou — from the blood of Abel to the blood of Zechariah, who was killed between the altar and the sanctuary. Abel (Gen 4) and Zechariah (2 Chronicles 24:20-21) are the first and last murder victims in the Hebrew canonical order (Genesis to Chronicles). The bookending of canonical history by two murders creates a comprehensive indictment: the history of prophetic rejection spans the entire Bible. Nai legō hymin, ekzētēthēsetai apo tēs geneas tautēs (yes I tell you, it will be required of this generation) — the generation witnessing Jesus is the terminus of the accumulated blood-guilt.
52: ouai hymin tois nomikois, hoti ērate tēn kleida tēs gnōseōs — woe to you experts in the law, because you have taken away the key to knowledge. Tēn kleida tēs gnōseōs (the key of knowledge) — the legal scholars possess the interpretive key to the Torah that should unlock the way to God; instead, they have locked the door. Autoi ouk eisēlthate kai tous eiserchomenous ekōlysate (you yourselves have not entered, and you have hindered those who were entering) — the scholars neither benefit from the key themselves nor allow others to benefit. Matthew’s parallel (Luke 23:13) speaks of shutting the kingdom of heaven in people’s faces; Luke’s “key of knowledge” emphasizes the epistemological function of the scribal tradition that has been weaponized against access to God.
53: kakeithen exelthontos autou ērxanto hoi grammateis kai hoi pharisaioi deinōs enechein kai apostomatizein auton peri pleionōn — when Jesus went outside, the Pharisees and teachers of the law began to oppose him fiercely and to besiege him with questions. Deinōs enechein (fiercely resenting, holding a deep grudge — the compound of deinōs terrible/fierce and enechein to hold/be seized by) conveys the intensity of the religious establishment’s response to the woes. Apostomatizein (to interrogate point by point, to question systematically — possibly to make him speak off the top of his head in order to catch him) is a forensic term: the opponents are constructing a case.
54: enedreouontes auton thēreusai ti ek tou stomatos autou — waiting to catch him in something he might say. Enedreouontes (lying in wait, setting an ambush — the ambush vocabulary of military or hunting) — the Pharisees and lawyers are now formally in the opposition role. Thēreusai (to hunt, to catch in a trap) is the hunter’s term applied to entrapment through speech. The chapter ends with the religious establishment in open, organized opposition — the trajectory toward the passion is now clearly established.
